definition: *Chthonic* describes what belongs to the earth and the underworld — its gods, powers, and rites, as against the bright sky-gods above. Greek religion drew a working line between the chthonic deities (Hades, Persephone, Demeter, Hekate) and the celestial Olympians; the word marks the darker, subterranean half of that divide.
history: From Greek *chthonios*, 'of the earth, underworld' (from *chthōn*, 'earth'). Greek and later magical practice split the divine into two registers: the celestial gods above, and the chthonic powers below, invoked for curses, binding spells, and dealings with the dead. Curse tablets and the Greek magical papyri routinely summon chthonic *daimones* — the underworld gods a magician addressed by night, often through the grave of one who died young. Ancient writers cast such rites as the shadow-side of state religion, appeals to the powers under the ground rather than the august sky.

def_sources.label: Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation
Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World

etymology.note: Greek religion split its gods: the chthonic earth-and-underworld powers (Hades, Demeter) against the bright Olympians above.
